message:
fix(conformal): empty-calibration score inflation + predict transition (bd-2tuel)

Two bugs in ConformalCalibrator:
1. conformity_score computed entity/event deviations against a default
   mean of 1.0 when calibration was empty, inflating scores for normal
   observations. Now returns violation-only component when n=0.
2. predict() returned None on the exact observation that met the
   min_calibration_samples threshold. Restructured to calibrate first,
   then produce a prediction if newly calibrated.

Also fixed conformal_calibration.rs E2E test: replaced non-existent
OracleSuite::observe() call with runtime.oracles.report().

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1gb7","title":"Epic: Ecosystem Parity - Complete Tokio Replacement","description":"# Epic: Ecosystem Parity - Complete Tokio Replacement

## Strategic Goal

Make Asupersync a **complete, drop-in replacement for the entire Tokio ecosystem**. After this epic, no one can say "Tokio can do X but Asupersync cannot."

## Background & Rationale

The Tokio ecosystem includes:
- **Core runtime**: tokio (scheduler, I/O, timers) - WE HAVE THIS
- **HTTP**: hyper, axum, warp - WE HAVE HTTP/1, HTTP/2, basic web framework
- **gRPC**: tonic - WE HAVE THIS
- **Database**: sqlx, diesel, sea-orm - AVAILABLE VIA RAID from sqlmodel_rust
- **WebSocket**: tokio-tungstenite - MISSING
- **Message queues**: async-nats, rdkafka, lapin - MISSING
- **Platform support**: Linux, macOS, Windows - PARTIAL (Linux only fully working)

This epic fills the remaining gaps while maintaining Asupersync's superiority:
- Cancel-correctness (no silent drops)
- Structured concurrency (no orphan tasks)
- Deterministic testing (lab runtime)
- Four-valued Outcomes (Ok/Err/Cancelled/Panicked)
- Obligation tracking (no resource leaks)

## What We're NOT Doing

- **Tokio interop**: We replace Tokio, we don't integrate with it
- **Compatibility layers**: No shims or adapters for Tokio code
- **Feature bloat**: Only what's needed for real applications

## Raid Strategy

Several components can be extracted from sibling projects:
- **sqlmodel_rust**: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite wire protocols + connection pooling
- **rich_rust**: Console rendering, progress bars, trace visualization
- **fastmcp_rust**: SSE transport, JSON-RPC codec

These should be extracted as standalone modules, not full dependencies.

## Sub-Epics

1. **WebSocket Protocol** (P1) - Real-time applications
2. **Platform Reactors** (P2) - macOS and Windows support
3. **Message Queue Clients** (P2) - Kafka, NATS, Redis
4. **HTTP/3 (QUIC)** (P2) - Future-proofing
5. **Async File I/O** (P2) - True async disk operations

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Every Tokio ecosystem capability has an Asupersync equivalent
- [ ] All implementations use structured concurrency
- [ ] All implementations are cancel-correct
- [ ] Lab runtime can test all components deterministically
- [ ] Documentation shows migration path from Tokio

## The Killer Response

After this epic, when someone says "But Tokio has X", we respond:

| They Say | We Say |
|----------|--------|
| "Tokio has WebSockets" | "So do we, with cancel-correct close handshakes" |
| "Tokio works on macOS/Windows" | "So do we, with native kqueue/IOCP" |
| "Tokio has Kafka/NATS/Redis" | "So do we, with obligation-tracked connections" |
| "Tokio has HTTP/3" | "So do we, with proper QUIC cancellation" |
| "Tokio is battle-tested" | "We have deterministic testing that PROVES correctness" |

## References

- asupersync_plan_v4.md: Core design principles
- Tokio ecosystem: https://tokio.rs/
- quinn (QUIC): https://github.com/quinn-rs/quinn
- WebSocket RFC 6455: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455

 {"id":"bd-2aiq","title":"Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients - NATS/Redis/Kafka Integration","description":"# Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients

## Overview
Implements first-party clients for major message queue systems (NATS, Redis Pub/Sub,
Kafka) with full Cx integration, structured concurrency, and cancel-correct semantics.

## Background & Motivation
Anyone using Tokio will say 'I can use async-nats, rdkafka, redis-rs for message queues.'
We need first-party, cancel-correct alternatives that integrate seamlessly with
Asupersync's structured concurrency model.

## Key Differentiators from Tokio Ecosystem

### Cancel-Correct Consumption
In Tokio, if a task consuming messages is cancelled, messages may be lost.
In Asupersync:
- Message delivery is an obligation
- Consumption must be ack/nack/committed/aborted
- Cancellation drains in-flight handlers gracefully
- No 'message lost because task dropped'

### Backpressure as First-Class Protocol
- Explicit credit windows
- Bounded queues with budget integration
- Producer slowdown when consumers lag

### Structured Lifecycle
- Connection owned by region
- Region close gracefully disconnects
- Child tasks for message handlers scoped to region

## Message Queue Clients

### 1. NATS Client
- Pure Rust implementation (no C dependencies)
- Subject-based pub/sub
- Request-reply pattern
- JetStream support (durable streams)

### 2. Redis Client
- Redis protocol (RESP)
- GET/SET/HGET/etc. operations
- Pub/Sub subscriptions
- Streams (XREAD, XADD, XACK)

### 3. Kafka Client
- Producer with exactly-once semantics
- Consumer groups with commit offsets
- Transactional support
- (May wrap librdkafka for protocol complexity)

## Integration Points
- All clients use Asupersync TCP/TLS stack
- Cx checkpoints for cancellation
- Budget integration for rate limiting
- Tracing for observability

## Tasks in This Sub-Epic
1. NATS client core (connect, pub/sub)
2. NATS JetStream integration
3. Redis client core (commands, connection)
4. Redis Pub/Sub and Streams
5. Kafka producer
6. Kafka consumer
7. Message queue E2E tests

 {"id":"bd-2tnl","title":"QUIC streams: bidirectional and unidirectional stream handling","description":"# QUIC Streams Implementation

## Overview
Implement QUIC stream handling with cancel-correct semantics for both
bidirectional and unidirectional streams.

## Design

### QuicConnection
```rust
pub struct QuicConnection {
    inner: quinn::Connection,
    streams: StreamTracker,
}

impl QuicConnection {
    /// Open bidirectional stream.
    pub async fn open_bi(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Outcome<(SendStream, RecvStream), QuicError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        
        let (send, recv) = self.inner.open_bi().await?;
        self.streams.register_bi(send.id());
        
        Ok((
            SendStream::new(send, &self.streams),
            RecvStream::new(recv, &self.streams),
        ))
    }
    
    /// Open unidirectional stream.
    pub async fn open_uni(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<SendStream, QuicError>;
    
    /// Accept bidirectional stream.
    pub async fn accept_bi(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Outcome<(SendStream, RecvStream), QuicError>;
    
    /// Accept unidirectional stream.
    pub async fn accept_uni(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<RecvStream, QuicError>;
    
    /// Close connection gracefully.
    pub async fn close(&self, cx: &Cx, code: u32, reason: &[u8]) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
}
```

### SendStream
```rust
pub struct SendStream {
    inner: quinn::SendStream,
    tracker: Arc<StreamTracker>,
}

impl SendStream {
    /// Write data to stream.
    pub async fn write(&mut self, cx: &Cx, data: &[u8]) -> Outcome<usize, QuicError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        Ok(self.inner.write(data).await?)
    }
    
    /// Write all data.
    pub async fn write_all(&mut self, cx: &Cx, data: &[u8]) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
    
    /// Finish sending (half-close).
    pub async fn finish(&mut self) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
    
    /// Reset stream with error code.
    pub fn reset(&mut self, code: u32);
}
```

### RecvStream
```rust
pub struct RecvStream {
    inner: quinn::RecvStream,
    tracker: Arc<StreamTracker>,
}

impl RecvStream {
    /// Read data from stream.
    pub async fn read(&mut self, cx: &Cx, buf: &mut [u8]) -> Outcome<Option<usize>, QuicError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        Ok(self.inner.read(buf).await?)
    }
    
    /// Read exact amount.
    pub async fn read_exact(&mut self, cx: &Cx, buf: &mut [u8]) -> Outcome<(), QuicError>;
    
    /// Read to end.
    pub async fn read_to_end(&mut self, cx: &Cx, limit: usize) -> Outcome<Vec<u8>, QuicError>;
    
    /// Stop reading with error code.
    pub fn stop(&mut self, code: u32);
}
```

## Cancellation Semantics
- SendStream reset with STOP_SENDING on cancel
- RecvStream stopped on cancel
- Connection tracks all streams for cleanup
- No data loss for already-transmitted frames

- DEBUG: Stream open/close

- WARN: Stream errors","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:22:47.778350386Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T01:56:21.472267793Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T01:56:21.472183997Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","quic"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2tnl","depends_on_id":"bd-10y0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:10Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2tnl","depends_on_id":"bd-2vik","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:10Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2un5","title":"Sub-Epic: Platform Reactors - Cross-Platform I/O Support","description":"# Sub-Epic: Platform Reactors

## Overview
Implements native I/O reactors for macOS (kqueue), Windows (IOCP), and Linux epoll
fallback, ensuring Asupersync runs efficiently on all major platforms.

## Background & Motivation
Currently Asupersync only has io_uring support (Linux 5.1+). Anyone saying 'Tokio
works on macOS and Windows' would be right that we don't. This sub-epic fixes that.

## Current State
- io_uring: Implemented (Linux 5.1+)
- epoll: Partial/fallback for older Linux
- kqueue: Not implemented (macOS)
- IOCP: Stub only (Windows)

## Platform Reactor Architecture
```rust
/// Unified reactor trait for all platforms.
pub trait Reactor: Send + Sync {
    /// Register interest in a file descriptor/handle.
    fn register(&self, source: &impl AsRawFd, interest: Interest) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Modify interest for a registered source.
    fn reregister(&self, source: &impl AsRawFd, interest: Interest) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Deregister a source.
    fn deregister(&self, source: &impl AsRawFd) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Wait for events with optional timeout.
    fn poll(&self, events: &mut Events, timeout: Option<Duration>) -> io::Result<usize>;
}

/// Platform-specific reactor selection.
#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Box<dyn Reactor>> {
    // Try io_uring first, fall back to epoll
    if io_uring::is_available() {
        Ok(Box::new(IoUringReactor::new()?))
    } else {
        Ok(Box::new(EpollReactor::new()?))
    }
}

#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Box<dyn Reactor>> {
    Ok(Box::new(KqueueReactor::new()?))
}

#[cfg(target_os = "windows")]
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Box<dyn Reactor>> {
    Ok(Box::new(IocpReactor::new()?))
}
```

## Tasks in This Sub-Epic
1. Reactor trait unification (common interface)
2. kqueue reactor (macOS)
3. IOCP reactor (Windows)
4. epoll fallback (older Linux)
5. Platform reactor E2E tests

## Integration Points
- IoDriver uses platform reactor transparently
- No user-facing API changes
- Lab runtime mock reactor unchanged

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All major platforms supported (Linux, macOS, Windows)
- [ ] Unified Reactor trait for all backends
- [ ] Automatic best-reactor selection per platform
- [ ] No performance regression on Linux io_uring
- [ ] E2E tests pass on all platforms
- [ ] CI matrix includes macOS and Windows","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T01:19:43.413890928Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.013481111Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.013377639Z","close_reason":"All children completed","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","platform"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2un5","depends_on_id":"bd-1gb7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:10Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2usq","title":"HPACK: recursive decoding stack overflow (FIXED)","description":"# Bug Fix: HPACK Recursive Stack Overflow (COMPLETED)

## Status: FIXED in session 2026-01-31

## Location
`src/http/h2/hpack.rs` - decode_header_block

## Vulnerability
The decoder called itself recursively for dynamic table size updates.
Malicious input with many consecutive size updates could cause stack overflow.

## Fix Applied
Converted to iterative approach with MAX_SIZE_UPDATES limit:

```rust
const MAX_SIZE_UPDATES: usize = 16;

fn decode_header_block(&mut self, data: &[u8]) -> Result<Headers, H2Error> {
    let mut size_updates = 0;
    
    loop {
        if data[pos] & 0xe0 == 0x20 {
            // Dynamic table size update
            size_updates += 1;
            if size_updates > MAX_SIZE_UPDATES {
                return Err(H2Error::compression("too many size updates"));
            }
            // Process size update iteratively
            continue;
        }
        // Normal header processing
        break;
    }
}
```

### Loom Tests

```rust
#[cfg(loom)]
mod loom_tests {
    use super::*;
    use loom::sync::atomic::{AtomicBool, AtomicU32, Ordering};
    use loom::thread;

    #[test]
    fn loom_parker_no_lost_wakeup() {
        loom::model(|| {
            let parker = Arc::new(Parker::new());
            let woken = Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(false));
            
            let p1 = parker.clone();
            let w1 = woken.clone();
            let h1 = thread::spawn(move || {
                p1.park();
                w1.store(true, Ordering::Release);
            });
            
            parker.unpark();
            h1.join().unwrap();
            
            assert!(woken.load(Ordering::Acquire), "Lost wakeup!");
        });
    }

    #[test]
    fn loom_parker_unpark_before_park() {
        loom::model(|| {
            let parker = Arc::new(Parker::new());
            
            // Unpark first
            parker.unpark();
            
            // Park should return immediately
            let p1 = parker.clone();
            let h1 = thread::spawn(move || {
                p1.park(); // Should not block
            });
            
            h1.join().unwrap();
        });
    }

    #[test]
    fn loom_wake_state_no_double_schedule() {
        loom::model(|| {
            let wake_state = Arc::new(AtomicU32::new(IDLE));
            let schedule_count = Arc::new(AtomicU32::new(0));
            
            let ws1 = wake_state.clone();
            let sc1 = schedule_count.clone();
            let h1 = thread::spawn(move || {
                let prev = ws1.swap(IDLE, Ordering::AcqRel);
                if prev == NOTIFIED {
                    sc1.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
                }
            });
            
            let ws2 = wake_state.clone();
            let sc2 = schedule_count.clone();
            thread::spawn(move || {
                let prev = ws2.swap(NOTIFIED, Ordering::AcqRel);
                if prev == IDLE {
                    sc2.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
                }
            }).join().unwrap();
            
            h1.join().unwrap();
            assert_eq!(schedule_count.load(Ordering::Relaxed), 1);
        });
    }

    #[test]
    fn loom_local_queue_steal_concurrent() {
        loom::model(|| {
            let queue = Arc::new(LocalQueue::new());
            
            // Producer pushes
            let q1 = queue.clone();
            let h1 = thread::spawn(move || {
                for i in 0..3 {
                    q1.push(Task::new(i));
                }
            });
            
            // Stealer steals
            let q2 = queue.clone();
            let stolen = Arc::new(AtomicU32::new(0));
            let s = stolen.clone();
            let h2 = thread::spawn(move || {
                while let Some(_) = q2.steal() {
                    s.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
                }
            });
            
            h1.join().unwrap();
            h2.join().unwrap();
            
            // All tasks either in queue or stolen
            let remaining = queue.len();
            let stolen_count = stolen.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
            assert_eq!(remaining + stolen_count, 3);
        });
    }

    #[test]
    fn loom_global_queue_mpsc() {
        loom::model(|| {
            let queue = Arc::new(GlobalQueue::new());
            
            let q1 = queue.clone();
            let h1 = thread::spawn(move || {
                q1.push(Task::new(1));
            });
            
            let q2 = queue.clone();
            let h2 = thread::spawn(move || {
                q2.push(Task::new(2));
            });
            
            h1.join().unwrap();
            h2.join().unwrap();
            
            // Both tasks present
            assert_eq!(queue.len(), 2);
        });
    }
}
```

Enhance Tower adapters with graceful degradation when asupersync features aren't available and provide fallback behavior.

## Requirements

### Graceful Degradation
When wrapping Tower services that don't support cancellation:

```rust
pub struct AsupersyncAdapter<S> {
    inner: S,
    config: AdapterConfig,
}

pub struct AdapterConfig {
    /// How to handle Tower services that ignore cancellation.
    pub cancellation_mode: CancellationMode,
    
    /// Timeout for non-cancellable operations.
    pub fallback_timeout: Option<Duration>,
}

pub enum CancellationMode {
    /// Best effort: set cancelled flag, but operation may complete.
    BestEffort,
    
    /// Strict: fail if service doesn't respect cancellation.
    Strict,
    
    /// Timeout: cancel via timeout if Cx cancelled.
    TimeoutFallback,
}
```

### Error Mapping
Map between Tower and asupersync error types:

```rust
pub trait ErrorAdapter {
    type TowerError;
    type AsupersyncError;
    
    fn to_asupersync(&self, err: Self::TowerError) -> Self::AsupersyncError;
    fn to_tower(&self, err: Self::AsupersyncError) -> Self::TowerError;
}

// Default implementation
impl<E: Into<Box<dyn Error>>> ErrorAdapter for DefaultErrorAdapter<E> {
    // ...
}
```

### Load Shedding Integration
Support Tower's load shedding with asupersync budget system:

```rust
impl<S> AsupersyncService<Request> for AsupersyncAdapter<S>
where
    S: tower::Service<Request> + Clone,
{
    async fn call(&self, cx: &Cx, request: Request) -> Result<Self::Response, Self::Error> {
        // Check Tower service readiness
        if !self.inner.poll_ready().is_ready() {
            // Respect Cx budget - fail fast if low budget
            if cx.remaining_budget() < MIN_BUDGET_FOR_WAIT {
                return Err(Overloaded);
            }
        }
        // ...
    }
}
```

## Overview
Configurable runtime builder with sensible defaults and tuning options.

## RuntimeConfig

```rust
pub struct RuntimeConfig {
    /// Number of worker threads (default: num_cpus)
    pub worker_threads: usize,
    
    /// Stack size per worker thread (default: 2MB)
    pub thread_stack_size: usize,
    
    /// Name prefix for worker threads
    pub thread_name_prefix: String,
    
    /// Global queue size limit (0 = unbounded)
    pub global_queue_limit: usize,
    
    /// Work stealing batch size
    pub steal_batch_size: usize,
    
    /// Blocking pool configuration
    pub blocking: BlockingPoolConfig,
    
    /// Enable parking lot for idle workers
    pub enable_parking: bool,
    
    /// Time slice for cooperative yielding (polls)
    pub poll_budget: u32,
    
    /// Callbacks
    pub on_thread_start: Option<Arc<dyn Fn() + Send + Sync>>,
    pub on_thread_stop: Option<Arc<dyn Fn() + Send + Sync>>,
}

impl Default for RuntimeConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            worker_threads: num_cpus::get(),
            thread_stack_size: 2 * 1024 * 1024,
            thread_name_prefix: "asupersync-worker".to_string(),
            global_queue_limit: 0,
            steal_batch_size: 16,
            blocking: BlockingPoolConfig::default(),
            enable_parking: true,
            poll_budget: 128,
            on_thread_start: None,
            on_thread_stop: None,
        }
    }
}
```

```rust
        self
    }

        self.config.thread_name_prefix = prefix.into();

    }
    
    pub fn blocking_threads(mut self, min: usize, max: usize) -> Self {
        self.config.blocking.min_threads = min;
        self.config.blocking.max_threads = max;
        self
    }
    
    pub fn on_thread_start<F>(mut self, f: F) -> Self
    where

        self
    }

        Runtime::with_config(self.config)
```

 {"id":"bd-cbz","title":"Implement two-phase mutex with guard obligations","description":"## Purpose
Implement a cancel-safe async mutex where lock guards are obligations. This ensures locks are always released even under cancellation.

## Why Async Mutex?
Async code cannot hold `std::sync::Mutex` guards across await points (not Send in general). Async mutex allows:
- Holding lock across await
- Cancel-safe lock acquisition
- Obligation tracking for lock release

## Two-Phase Mutex Model

```rust
pub struct Mutex<T> {
    locked: AtomicBool,
    waiters: WaitQueue,
    data: UnsafeCell<T>,
}

pub struct MutexGuard<'a, T> {
    mutex: &'a Mutex<T>,
    obligation_id: ObligationId,
}

impl<T> Mutex<T> {
    /// Create new mutex.
    pub fn new(value: T) -> Self;
    
    /// Lock the mutex. Cancel-safe during wait.
    pub async fn lock(&self, cx: &mut Cx<'_>) -> MutexGuard<'_, T>;
    
    /// Try to lock without waiting.
    pub fn try_lock(&self) -> Option<MutexGuard<'_, T>>;
    
    /// Get mutable reference (if exclusively owned).
    pub fn get_mut(&mut self) -> &mut T;
}

impl<T> Deref for MutexGuard<'_, T> {
    type Target = T;
    fn deref(&self) -> &T { /* ... */ }
}

impl<T> DerefMut for MutexGuard<'_, T> {
    fn deref_mut(&mut self) -> &mut T { /* ... */ }
}

impl<T> Drop for MutexGuard<'_, T> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // Unlock mutex
        // Resolve obligation as Committed
        // Wake next waiter
    }
}
```

## Guard as Obligation
Like semaphore permits, mutex guards are obligations:
- **Created**: When lock acquired
- **Committed**: When guard dropped (unlocked)

## Two-Phase Semantics
- **Phase 1**: Wait for lock availability (cancel-safe)
- **Phase 2**: Acquire lock (creates obligation)

Cancellation during wait is clean - no lock held.

## Fairness and Priority
Options for lock scheduling:
1. **FIFO**: Waiters serviced in order (prevents starvation)
2. **Priority**: Higher priority tasks get lock first
3. **Barging**: New arrivals can barge if lucky (not recommended)

For Asupersync, FIFO is default. Priority can be added via separate API.

## Deadlock Prevention
The spec does not prevent deadlocks at runtime, but:
- Lab runtime can detect deadlock (cycle in wait graph)
- Timeout wrappers can bound wait time
- Design patterns (lock ordering) prevent in application

## Common Pattern: Shared State
```rust
let state = Mutex::new(SharedState::default());

scope.spawn(cx, |cx| async move {
    let mut guard = state.lock(cx).await;
    guard.counter += 1;
    // guard dropped, lock released
});
```

## Cancellation Handling
| Scenario | Behavior |
|----------|----------|
| Cancel during lock wait | Clean abort, lock not held |
| Cancel while holding lock | Guard dropped, lock released |
| Panic while holding lock | Guard dropped (unwind safety) |

## Invariant Support
- **Obligation tracking**: Guards are obligations
- **No deadlock leaks**: Guards always release on drop
- **Cancel-safety**: Wait is interruptible

## Testing Requirements
1. Basic lock/unlock
2. Contention (multiple waiters)
3. Cancel during wait
4. try_lock success and failure
5. FIFO ordering verification
6. Deadlock detection (lab runtime)
7. Guard deref operations

## References
- asupersync_plan_v4.md: §6.5 Two-Phase Operations
- tokio::sync::Mutex
- async-std::sync::Mutex
- parking_lot mutex

## Acceptance Criteria
- Mutex acquisition uses a two-phase / obligation-based protocol so cancellation cannot silently lose ownership.
- Dropping a guard/permit has deterministic semantics (release/abort) and is trace-visible.
- Unit/E2E tests cover cancellation while waiting, while holding the guard, and region close interactions.

 {"id":"bd-cdmp","title":"Implement trace file format and serialization","description":"## Overview

Implement the binary file format for persisting recorded traces and loading them for replay.

## Background

Traces need to be saved to disk for later replay. The format should be:
- Compact (traces can be large for long tests)
- Versioned (forward compatible as schema evolves)
- Streamable (can start replay before full load)

## File Format Design

### Header
```
Magic: "ASUPERTRACE" (11 bytes)
Version: u16 (format version)
Flags: u16 (compression, etc.)
Metadata length: u32
Metadata: TraceMetadata (bincode)
```

### Events Section
```
Event count: u64
Events: [TraceEvent] (bincode, optionally compressed)
```

### Optional Compression
- Support LZ4 compression for large traces
- Compression flag in header
- Chunk-based for streaming decompression

## API

```rust
pub struct TraceFile {
    path: PathBuf,
}

impl TraceFile {
    pub fn create(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<TraceWriter>;
    pub fn open(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<TraceReader>;
}

pub struct TraceWriter { /* ... */ }
impl TraceWriter {
    pub fn write_metadata(&mut self, meta: &TraceMetadata) -> io::Result<()>;
    pub fn write_event(&mut self, event: &TraceEvent) -> io::Result<()>;
    pub fn finish(self) -> io::Result<()>;
}

pub struct TraceReader { /* ... */ }
impl TraceReader {
    pub fn metadata(&self) -> &TraceMetadata;
    pub fn events(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = io::Result<TraceEvent>>;
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] File format is documented
- [ ] Version field allows future schema changes
- [ ] Round-trip test: write then read preserves all data
- [ ] Large trace test (10k+ events) works correctly
- [ ] Optional compression reduces file size by 50%+","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CalmMeadow","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T18:01:29.962338187Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-20T23:52:39.351262439Z","closed_at":"2026-01-20T23:52:39.349802790Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cdmp","depends_on_id":"bd-u8yo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:01:55Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-cl94","title":"Add health checking and async factory to resource pool","description":"## Overview

Enhance resource pool with connection health validation, warmup strategy, and async factory support.

## Requirements

### Health Checking
```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait Pool: Send + Sync {
    // ... existing ...
    
    /// Check if a resource is still healthy/usable.
    /// Called before returning from idle pool.
    async fn health_check(&self, resource: &Self::Resource) -> bool {
        true  // Default: assume healthy
    }
}

pub struct PoolConfig {
    // ... existing ...
    
    /// Health check before returning idle resources.
    pub health_check_on_acquire: bool,
    
    /// Periodic health check interval for idle resources.
    pub health_check_interval: Option<Duration>,
    
    /// Remove unhealthy resources immediately.
    pub evict_unhealthy: bool,
}
```

### Connection Warmup
```rust
pub struct PoolConfig {
    // ... existing ...
    
    /// Pre-create this many connections on pool init.
    pub warmup_connections: usize,
    
    /// Timeout for warmup phase.
    pub warmup_timeout: Duration,
    
    /// Strategy when warmup partially fails.
    pub warmup_failure_strategy: WarmupStrategy,
}

pub enum WarmupStrategy {
    /// Continue with whatever connections succeeded.
    BestEffort,
    /// Fail pool creation if any warmup fails.
    FailFast,
    /// Require at least min_connections.
    RequireMinimum,
}
```

### Async Factory
```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait AsyncResourceFactory: Send + Sync {
    type Resource: Send;
    type Error: Error;
    
    async fn create(&self) -> Result<Self::Resource, Self::Error>;
    
    /// Optional: cleanup before resource is dropped.
    async fn destroy(&self, resource: Self::Resource) -> Result<(), Self::Error> {
        drop(resource);
        Ok(())
    }
}

impl<R, F> GenericPool<R, F>
where
    F: AsyncResourceFactory<Resource = R>,
{
    pub fn new(factory: F, config: PoolConfig) -> Self;
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
1. health_check() in Pool trait with default impl
2. Configurable health check timing
3. Warmup with configurable strategy
4. Async factory trait
5. Tests for unhealthy resource eviction

- Test warmup creates min_connections
- Test warmup timeout handling
- Test async factory with cancellation
